1: šomrēnî ʾēl kî ḥāsîtî bāk (šomrēnî ʾēl kî ḥāsîṯî ḇāḵ): 'Preserve me, O God, for I take refuge in you.' The miktam superscription (miktam lĕdāwid) is of uncertain meaning — possibly 'inscription' or related to ketam ('gold'), suggesting a precious composition. The qal imperative šomrēnî ('guard, keep, preserve') from šāmar invokes the divine watchfulness that is YHWH's primary covenant function (Num 6:24; Ps 121:7-8). The ground kî ḥāsîtî bāk ('because I have taken refuge in you') — the ḥāsâ root — places the psalmist entirely on YHWH's protective provision rather than any alternative.
2: ʾāmart laYHWH ʾădōnāy ʾātâ (ʾāmartî laYHWH ʾăḏōnāy ʾāttâ): 'I say to the LORD, 'You are my Lord.'' The confession ʾădōnāy ʾātâ ('you are my Lord/Master') is the foundational sovereignty declaration. The phrase ṭôbātî bal-ʿālêkā, 'my good is not beyond you / my good has no reference point above you,' affirms that all genuine blessing is located in and derived from YHWH. LXX renders this with some difficulty; the MT is also textually challenging. But the theological point is clear: YHWH is the psalmist's supreme good, not a means to other goods.
3: liqdōšîm ʾăšer bāʾāreṣ hēmmâ (liqḏôšîm ʾăšer-bāʾāreṣ hēmmâ): 'As for the holy ones in the land, they are the excellent ones.' The referent of qĕdôšîm ('holy ones') is debated: foreign gods/their devotees, or faithful Israelites. The following phrase wĕʾaddîrê kol-ḥepṣî bām ('and the noble/majestic ones, all my delight is in them') and v.4's contrast with those who 'run after another' suggests the qĕdôšîm here are the faithful covenant community. The psalmist's delight is with this community — a social theology of belonging to the people of YHWH.
4: yirbû ʿaṣṣĕbôtām ʾaḥēr māhārû (yirbû ʿaṣṣĕḇôṯām ʾaḥēr māhārû): 'The sorrows of those who run after another god shall multiply.' The noun ʿaṣṣābôt ('sorrows, idols') — related to ʿeṣeb ('pain/idol') — captures the double meaning: idol worship produces pain. The phrase ʾaḥēr māhārû, 'they run after another,' uses ʾaḥēr ('another') for foreign deities — a pejorative, refusing them divine titles. The psalmist's triple refusal — no drink offerings of blood, no taking their names on his lips, no offering — constitutes comprehensive rejection of the rival cult.
5: YHWH mĕnat-ḥelqî wĕkôsî (YHWH mĕnaṯ-ḥelqî wĕḵôsî): 'The LORD is my chosen portion and my cup.' The nouns ḥēleq ('portion, allotment') and kôs ('cup') together describe the totality of inheritance. In the land distribution (Josh 13-21), each tribe received a ḥēleq; Levi's portion was YHWH himself (Deut 10:9; 18:2). By claiming YHWH as his ḥēleq, the psalmist spiritualizes the levitical ideal: anyone may choose YHWH as the substance of their inheritance. The phrase ʾattâ tômîk gôrālî, 'you hold my lot,' pictures YHWH himself holding the lot-cast that determines the psalmist's future.
6: ḥăbālîm nāplû-lî binnĕʿīmîm (ḥăḇālîm nāplû-lî ḇannĕʿîmîm): 'The lines have fallen for me in pleasant places.' The noun ḥebel ('measuring line') refers to land surveying — the lines marking one's lot. The passive nāplû ('have fallen') is the language of lot-casting: the result of throwing the lot is that his portion lies in pleasant territory. The phrase ʾap-naḥălat šāpĕrâ ʿālay, 'indeed, I have a beautiful inheritance,' uses the rare verb šāpar ('to be beautiful/fair'). When YHWH is one's portion (v.5), the inheritance is inherently beautiful — the psalm's meditation on contentment grounded in divine choice.
7: ʾăbārēk ʾet-YHWH ʾăšer yĕʿāṣānî (ʾăḇārēḵ ʾeṯ-YHWH ʾăšer yĕʿāṣānî): 'I bless the LORD who gives me counsel.' The verb yāʿaṣ ('to counsel, advise') describes YHWH as the psalmist's wisdom-source. The phrase ʾap-lêlôt yissĕrûnî kilĕyôtāy, 'even in the night my kidneys (reins) instruct me,' attributes the body's night-time processing to divine instruction: the kidneys (kĕlāyôt), understood in Hebrew thought as the seat of deep feeling and moral intuition, function as an inner voice that continues YHWH's counsel in sleep. This nocturnal instruction anticipates the 'meditation day and night' of Ps 1:2.
8: šiwwîtî YHWH lĕnegdî tāmîd (šiwwîṯî YHWH lĕneḡḏî ṯāmîd): 'I have set the LORD always before me.' The verb šāwâ ('to set, place') in the piel šiwwîtî means 'I have placed/levelled before me as a standard.' The phrase kî mimînî bal-ʾemmôṭ, 'because he is at my right hand, I shall not be moved,' positions YHWH as the constant companion who prevents collapse. Peter cites vv.8-11 in Acts 2:25-28, arguing that David — who wrote this — did not die permanently (Acts 2:29), therefore the psalm speaks of the Messiah: Jesus who was raised from the dead is the true referent of the one who would not be moved.
9: lākēn śāmaḥ libbî wayyāgel kĕbôdî (lāḵēn śāmaḥ libbî wayyāḡel kĕḇôḏî): 'Therefore my heart is glad, and my glory rejoices.' The causal lākēn ('therefore') links the settled trust of v.8 to the threefold joy: heart (lēb), glory/honor (kābôd), and flesh (bāśār). The phrase ʾap-bĕśārî yiškon lābeṭaḥ, 'my flesh also rests in security,' is programmatic for Acts 2:26: Peter reads the resting of flesh in security as pointing beyond David's own confidence to the resurrection body of the Messiah — flesh that genuinely rested in the security of incorruption.
10: kî lōʾ-taʿăzōb napšî lišĕʾôl (kî lōʾ-taʿăzōḇ napšî lišĕʾôl): 'For you will not abandon my soul to Sheol.' The key word napšî ('my soul/life') — the whole self — will not be abandoned (ʿāzab) to Sheol (the realm of the dead). The parallelism: lōʾ-tittēn ḥăsîdĕkā lirʾôt šāḥat, 'nor will you give your Holy One to see the Pit.' The noun šāḥat ('pit, destruction') is parallel to Sheol. Peter's argument in Acts 2:27 is precise: since David died and his tomb is known (Acts 2:29), David could not have spoken of himself. The one who did not see corruption is Jesus, raised before his body could decay. Paul makes the same argument in Acts 13:35-37.
11: tôdîʿēnî ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm (tôḏîʿēnî ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm): 'You make known to me the path of life.' The hiphil tôdîʿēnî ('you cause me to know') from yādaʿ signals experiential, relational knowledge — not merely information about life but being shown its path. The phrase śōbaʿ śĕmāḥôt ʾet-pānêkā, 'fullness of joys with your face,' places the highest joy in the divine presence (pānêkā, 'your face'). The final phrase nĕʿīmôt bîmînĕkā nĕṣaḥ, 'pleasures at your right hand forevermore,' uses nĕṣaḥ ('forever') — the same word used of divine permanence in Ps 9:7. The path of life leads to perpetual divine presence: this verse grounds Christian eschatology in the Psalter's vision of beatific communion with YHWH.
